title: ServiceNow Notification Templates
content:
ServiceNow Notification Templates
      Modern HTML email templates to make your notifications stand out
      
      
        August 21, 2024
        
          ServiceNow
          Email Templates
        
      
    
    
    
      
        
          📋
          What's This About?
        
        
          
            01
            
              Tesla Style
              Minimalist & futuristic design
            
          
          
            02
            
              Google Style
              Material Design inspired
            
          
          
            03
            
              Adobe Style
              Editorial & professional
            
          
          
            04
            
              ChatGPT Style
              Modern & conversational
            
          
          
            05
            
              macOS Style
              Native & clean design
            
          
        
      
    
  

  
    
      ServiceNow notifications are crucial for keeping users informed, but let's be honest - the default styling could use some serious love. I've created 5 modern HTML templates that will make your notifications stand out and actually get read.
    

    
      Tesla Style - Minimalist & Futuristic
      Perfect for: System alerts, status updates, critical notifications
      
      The Tesla template embodies the sleek, minimalist aesthetic that Tesla is known for. Clean lines, subtle shadows, and a professional color scheme make this template perfect for system-level notifications.

      Key Features
      
        Clean white background with Tesla-inspired shadows
        Minimal color palette (white, black, Tesla red accents)
        Professional typography with excellent readability
        Smooth hover effects and transitions
        Responsive design that works on all devices
      

      Design Philosophy: Less is more. This template focuses on clarity and professionalism while maintaining visual appeal.

      
        
          Network connectivity issue affecting multiple users • INC0012345
        

        
          
            
              
                
                  
                    TESLA
                  
                

                
                  
                    

                      
                        
                          Network connectivity issue affecting multiple users
                        
                      

                      
                        
                          
                            In Progress

                                High
                                SLA Impact

Updates will be provided as they become available.

        Download Tesla Template
      
    

    
      Google Style - Material Design
      Perfect for: User communications, approval requests, general updates
      
      The Google template follows Material Design principles with clean cards, proper elevation, and Google's signature color palette. It's perfect for user-facing communications that need to feel familiar and trustworthy.

      Key Features
      
        Material Design card layout with proper elevation
        Google's color scheme (blue, red, yellow, green accents)
        Subtle shadows and smooth animations
        Accessible design patterns
        Professional yet approachable feel
      

      Design Philosophy: Familiar and trustworthy. Users instantly recognize and trust Google's design language.

      
        
          Software license renewal reminder • REQ0012345

      Adobe Style - Editorial & Professional
      Perfect for: Creative teams, design requests, content approvals
      
      The Adobe template features a clean editorial design with a signature red accent bar and professional typography. It's perfect for creative teams and design-related requests that need to feel polished and trustworthy.

      Key Features
      
        Editorial-style layout with clean typography
        Adobe's signature red accent bar
        Professional color scheme (white, black, red accents)
        Clean information hierarchy
        Subtle borders and spacing
      

      Design Philosophy: Editorial excellence. This template brings the same level of polish and professionalism that Adobe's products are known for.

      ChatGPT Style - Modern & Conversational
      Perfect for: AI-related requests, knowledge base updates, user support
      
      The ChatGPT template features a modern dark header with a conversational design approach. It uses timeline-style information display and ChatGPT's signature green color scheme, making it perfect for AI-related requests and knowledge management.

      Key Features
      
        Dark header with ChatGPT branding
        Timeline-style information display
        ChatGPT's signature green color scheme
        Modern rounded corners and shadows
        Conversational and approachable feel
      

      Design Philosophy: Modern and conversational. This template brings the same friendly, approachable feel that ChatGPT is known for.

      macOS Style - Native & Clean
      Perfect for: Internal communications, system notifications, general updates
      
      The macOS template mimics the native macOS interface with window controls, system fonts, and Apple's design language. It's perfect for internal communications that need to feel familiar and native to Mac users.

      Key Features
      
        Native macOS window design with traffic light controls
        Apple's system fonts and typography
        Property list-style information display
        macOS color scheme and spacing
        Clean, minimal interface design
      

      Design Philosophy: Native and familiar. This template brings the same clean, intuitive feel that macOS users know and love.

title: Building MCP Servers with Role-Based Access Control
content:
Building MCP Servers with Role-Based Access Control
      Giving AI assistants access to your tools without giving them access to everything

      
        March 5, 2025
        8 min read
        Advanced
        
          AI
          Security
        
      
    

    
      
        
          📋
          What's This About?
        
        
          
            01
            
              What Is MCP?
              The protocol connecting AI to your tools
            
          
          
            02
            
              Why RBAC Matters Here
              The access control problem nobody talks about
            
          
          
            03
            
              Anatomy of an MCP Server
              Tools, resources, and prompts
            
          
          
            04
            
              Building in Access Control
              Patterns that actually work
            
          
          
            05
            
              A ServiceNow Example
              Putting it together in practice
            
          
          
            06
            
              My Takeaway
              Where this is all going
            
          
        
      
    
  

  
    
      I've been spending a lot of late nights thinking about how AI assistants fit into enterprise tooling — specifically how to let them actually do useful things without handing them the keys to the kingdom. MCP (Model Context Protocol) is the protocol that makes this possible, and it's genuinely exciting. But every conversation I see about building MCP servers glosses over the part that matters most in a production environment: who is allowed to do what, and how do you enforce that when the requester is an AI?
    

    
      What Is MCP?
      Model Context Protocol is an open standard created by Anthropic that defines how AI models communicate with external tools and data sources. Think of it as a common language that lets an AI assistant — Claude, for example — call functions in your systems, read from your databases, trigger workflows, or query APIs in a structured, auditable way.

      Without MCP, every AI integration is a one-off: custom code, custom auth, custom error handling, no standard way for the AI to know what capabilities are available. With MCP, you build a server that exposes your tools in a standard way, and any MCP-compatible AI client can discover and use them.

      An MCP server exposes three types of things:

      
        Tools — callable functions the AI can invoke (create a ticket, query a database, send a notification)
        Resources — data the AI can read (a document, a record, a configuration file)
        Prompts — pre-built prompt templates the AI can use for specific tasks
      

      The AI client connects to your MCP server, discovers what's available, and then calls things as the user asks for them. That's the basic idea. Now here's where it gets interesting — and where most tutorials stop before the important part.
    

    
      Why RBAC Matters Here
      When you build an MCP server that connects to, say, ServiceNow, and you hand it a service account with admin rights so it can "do everything" — you've just given every person who uses that AI assistant admin-level access to ServiceNow, regardless of what their actual role is. That's a problem.

      The AI doesn't have its own intentions. It acts on behalf of the user who's prompting it. So if a user without Change Manager permissions asks the AI to "approve this change request," and your MCP server has admin rights, the AI will happily do it. The user never had that permission in the underlying system — they got it by going through the AI.

      This is the access control problem that doesn't get talked about enough in MCP conversations. The protocol itself is neutral — it doesn't tell you how to handle authorization. That's up to you. Role-Based Data Access Control (RBAC) in your MCP server is how you make sure the AI can only do what the human behind the prompt is actually allowed to do.

      
        The key principle: The AI assistant should never be able to perform an action on behalf of a user that the user couldn't perform themselves in the underlying system. Your MCP server enforces this.
      
    

    
      Anatomy of an MCP Server
      Before getting into the access control patterns, it helps to understand what an MCP server actually looks like. They're typically written in Python or TypeScript using the official MCP SDK. A minimal server looks something like this conceptually:

      
        You define your tools with names, descriptions, and input schemas
        Each tool has a handler function that gets called when the AI invokes it
        The server handles the MCP transport (stdio or HTTP/SSE) and routes calls to the right handlers
        The AI client connects, lists available tools, and calls them as needed
      

      The input schema is important — it's how the AI knows what parameters a tool expects. A well-defined schema means the AI can construct valid calls without guessing. A vague schema means the AI guesses, and guessing leads to errors.

      The handler function is where your business logic lives. This is also where your access control logic lives. The handler receives the request, knows who's calling (if you've wired up identity), and decides whether to execute or reject it.
    

    
      Building in Access Control
      There are a few patterns that work well for RBAC in MCP servers, depending on how your environment is set up.

      Pattern 1: Pass-Through Authentication
      The cleanest approach. Instead of using a service account, your MCP server accepts a user token (OAuth, API key, session token) from the client and passes it through to the underlying system. The underlying system — ServiceNow, your database, your API — enforces its own permissions on that token.

      This means the AI's access is exactly the same as the user's access. If the user can't approve changes in ServiceNow, the API call will fail when the AI tries it on their behalf. No extra access control layer needed on the MCP side because you're delegating to the system of record.

      The challenge: your AI client needs to securely forward user credentials to the MCP server, which requires some thought about the auth flow and token storage.

      Pattern 2: Role-Scoped Tool Visibility
      Instead of exposing all tools to all users and relying on downstream enforcement, your MCP server only surfaces the tools that the authenticated user is allowed to use. A help desk agent connecting to your ServiceNow MCP server sees tools like create_incident and update_incident. A Change Manager sees those plus approve_change and close_change. An admin sees everything.

      This is implemented in the tool listing response — the MCP server dynamically builds the list of available tools based on the authenticated identity. The AI can't call what it doesn't know exists.

      This is a stronger model because it prevents the AI from even attempting unauthorized actions. The pass-through model fails gracefully after the fact; this model prevents the attempt entirely.

      Pattern 3: Audit Everything
      Regardless of which pattern you use, every tool invocation should be logged with the calling user's identity, the tool name, the parameters passed, and the result. In an enterprise context, this isn't optional — it's how you prove the AI did what it was supposed to do, and it's how you investigate when something goes wrong.

      The MCP server is a great place to centralize this logging because every action flows through it. Build it in from the start; retrofitting audit logging is always messier than doing it upfront.
    

    
      A ServiceNow Example
      Here's how I think about this for a ServiceNow MCP server. ServiceNow already has a mature role-based access system — every API call is scoped to the user whose credentials are used. That makes it a natural fit for the pass-through authentication pattern.

      The MCP server would expose tools like:

      
        get_incident — fetch an incident by number or sys_id
        create_incident — open a new incident with provided details
        update_incident — update fields on an existing incident
        get_catalog_items — list available service catalog items
        submit_catalog_request — submit a catalog request on behalf of the user
        get_change_requests — query change requests with filters
        approve_change — approve a change request (Change Manager only)
      

      Each tool handler calls the ServiceNow REST API using the user's OAuth token. ServiceNow enforces its own ACLs on those calls — if the user doesn't have the itil role, create_incident will fail at the ServiceNow layer. If they don't have the change_manager role, approve_change will fail.

      On top of that, I'd layer role-scoped tool visibility — no point showing approve_change to someone who can't call it. And every call gets logged to a dedicated audit table with the user, tool, parameters, timestamp, and response status.

      The result: an AI assistant that can genuinely help with ServiceNow tasks — pulling up incidents, submitting requests, checking change status — without bypassing a single permission that ServiceNow enforces for human users.
    

    
      My Takeaway
      MCP is one of those things that feels like it's going to matter a lot in how enterprise IT operates over the next few years. The ability to give an AI assistant structured, auditable access to your internal tools — without building a custom integration for every single one — is genuinely powerful.

      But the "give it an admin service account and call it a day" approach is going to create problems. Access control in AI-powered tooling needs to be taken just as seriously as access control in any other system that touches your data. The AI acts on behalf of humans — it should be bounded by the same constraints those humans operate under.

      Build the RBAC in from the start. Log everything. Treat the MCP server as a security boundary, not just a translation layer. Do that, and you'll have something you can actually put in front of your organization with confidence.
    

    
      Get in Touch
    

    
      Helpful Resources
      
        
          MCP Official Docs
          The official Model Context Protocol documentation covering the spec, SDKs, and building your first server.
          Read the Docs →
        

        
          MCP Python SDK
          The official Python SDK for building MCP servers — includes quickstart examples and full API reference.
          View on GitHub →
        

        
          ServiceNow REST API Docs
          ServiceNow's REST API reference — the foundation for any ServiceNow MCP server implementation.
          Read Docs →
excerpt: MCP servers let AI assistants like Claude talk to your internal tools — but without access control, that's a security conversation you don't want to have. Here's how to build MCP servers with RBAC baked in from the start.

title: Immy.bot: The Endpoint Manager You Didn't Know You Needed
content:
Immy.bot: The Endpoint Manager You Didn't Know You Needed
      Smart software deployment that knows what's already on your machines

      
        February 10, 2025
        6 min read
        Beginner
        
          IT
          Automation
        
      
    

    
      
        
          📋
          What's This About?
        
        
          
            01
            
              What Is Immy.bot?
              Not just another RMM tool
            
          
          
            02
            
              How It Thinks
              Desired state vs. push and pray
            
          
          
            03
            
              Onboarding Sessions
              The feature that changes everything
            
          
          
            04
            
              The Software Library
              Curated, version-aware deployments
            
          
          
            05
            
              My Takeaway
              Who it's really for
            
          
        
      
    
  

  
    
      I've used a handful of RMM tools over the years and the story is usually the same: you pick a script, you push it, you hope it works, and then you spend the next hour troubleshooting why it installed the wrong version on half the machines. Immy.bot flips that model entirely. It doesn't just deploy software — it understands your endpoints well enough to know what should be there, what's already there, and what needs to change. That's a fundamentally different approach, and once you've used it, going back feels barbaric.
    

    
      What Is Immy.bot?
      Immy.bot is a SaaS endpoint management platform built primarily for MSPs and internal IT teams managing Windows environments. It handles software deployment, Windows updates, device onboarding, and recurring maintenance tasks — but the thing that makes it different is that it operates on a desired state model.

      Instead of saying "run this script on these machines," you say "all machines assigned to this client should have these applications at these versions, configured this way." Immy.bot figures out what's already there, compares it against what should be there, and only does what's actually necessary.

      It's not an RMM. It integrates with your existing RMM (ConnectWise Automate, NinjaRMM, Datto, and others) and uses that as a transport layer to reach endpoints. It also integrates with PSA tools like ConnectWise Manage and HaloPSA so you can tie deployments back to your clients and ticketing. Think of it as the orchestration layer that sits on top of your existing tools and makes them smarter.
    

    
      How It Thinks
      This is the part that took me a moment to fully appreciate. Most deployment tools ask: "Did the script run?" Immy.bot asks: "Is the machine in the state it should be in?"

      When Immy.bot evaluates an endpoint, it runs a detection script for each piece of software — not to install it, but just to check what's there and what version it is. Based on that, it decides whether to install, update, reinstall, or do nothing. The detection and the installation are separate steps, and that separation matters.

      This means:

      
        If a machine already has the right version installed, Immy skips it. No unnecessary reinstalls.
        If a machine has an older version, Immy updates it. Not reinstalls from scratch — updates.
        If a machine has a newer version than what's specified, Immy can be configured to leave it alone or downgrade, depending on your policy.
        If the software is present but misconfigured, Immy can run a configuration script on top of it without touching the installation.
      

      That granularity is what makes it feel less like a script runner and more like something that actually understands your environment.

      
        The big difference: Traditional RMM deployments are fire-and-forget. Immy.bot is idempotent — you can run it a hundred times and it'll only make changes when something is actually out of state.
      
    

    
      Onboarding Sessions
      If I had to pick one feature that makes Immy.bot worth it, it's Onboarding Sessions. This is the workflow that runs when a new device shows up in your environment.

      When you connect a new machine to Immy.bot, you trigger an onboarding session. Immy walks through a checklist: it detects the machine, identifies the tenant it belongs to (based on your integrations), and then presents you with a screen showing everything it's about to do. You can review the list, adjust anything, and then kick it off.

      The session then runs through every software and configuration assignment tied to that tenant — installing what needs to be installed, configuring what needs to be configured, running Windows updates, setting up any standard tooling. All in one automated flow.

      What this replaces: the person on the help desk who has a checklist doc in a shared drive, manually installing things one by one, occasionally forgetting step 7, and then getting a ticket three days later because the VPN client isn't there. Onboarding sessions replace that entire process with something repeatable and consistent every single time.
    

    
      The Software Library
      Immy.bot maintains a global software library — a curated collection of popular applications with pre-built detection scripts, installation scripts, and upgrade logic already written and maintained by the Immy team. You don't have to figure out how to silently install Zoom or detect what version of 7-Zip is present. It's already there.

      The library covers the usual suspects — Adobe Reader, Chrome, Firefox, Microsoft 365, VPN clients, monitoring agents, and a lot more. Each entry in the library stays updated as new versions are released, so when Adobe drops a security patch, the library gets updated and Immy knows there's a newer version it can push out.

      For software that's not in the global library — proprietary tools, internal apps, niche utilities — you can write your own software definitions using PowerShell. The detection script and installation script pattern is straightforward, and once you've written a few you get the hang of it quickly.

      You can also set version pinning per tenant. If one client needs to stay on an older version of something for compatibility reasons, you pin them while everyone else gets updated. That kind of per-tenant control without managing separate scripts for each client is genuinely useful at scale.
    

    
      My Takeaway
      Immy.bot is squarely aimed at MSPs, and it shows — the multi-tenant architecture, the PSA and RMM integrations, the per-client software assignments. But even if you're managing a single internal environment, the onboarding session workflow and the desired state model are compelling enough to justify it.

      The pricing model is per-endpoint-per-month, which adds up if you're managing thousands of machines, but for mid-size environments the time savings on onboarding alone tend to justify the cost pretty fast.

      If you've ever sat at a desk doing a manual Windows setup while a checklist doc mocks you from the other monitor — Immy.bot is the fix. It won't replace your RMM, but it'll make your deployments smarter and your onboarding process something you're not embarrassed to talk about.
    

    
      Get in Touch
    

    
      Helpful Resources
      
        
          Immy.bot Official Site
          The main site with pricing, feature overview, and a link to request a demo or start a trial.
          Visit Immy.bot →
        

        
          Immy.bot Docs
          Full documentation covering software definitions, onboarding sessions, integrations, and scripting reference.
          Read the Docs →
        

        
          Immy.bot Community
          The Discord community where MSPs share scripts, workflows, and tips for getting the most out of the platform.
          Join Community →
excerpt: Immy.bot is the endpoint management tool that actually gets onboarding right — software deployments that are smart enough to know what's already installed, what version it is, and whether it needs to be there at all.

title: ServiceNow Build Agent: CI/CD for Your Instance
content:
ServiceNow Build Agent: CI/CD for Your Instance
      Stop manually promoting apps and let the pipeline do the heavy lifting

      
        January 15, 2025
        7 min read
        Intermediate
        
          ServiceNow
          DevOps
        
      
    

    
      
        
          📋
          What's This About?
        
        
          
            01
            
              What Is the Build Agent?
              The problem it solves
            
          
          
            02
            
              How It Actually Works
              Pipelines, stages, and environments
            
          
          
            03
            
              Getting It Set Up
              What you need to get started
            
          
          
            04
            
              Gotchas I've Hit
              Things that will catch you off guard
            
          
          
            05
            
              My Takeaway
              Is it worth it?
            
          
        
      
    
  

  
    
      For years, promoting a ServiceNow application meant opening an Update Set, exporting an XML file, logging into your next environment, importing it, previewing it, committing it, and then holding your breath. If you had three environments — dev, test, prod — you did that dance twice every single deployment. The Build Agent is ServiceNow's answer to that pain, and once you actually get it running, you'll wonder how you lived without it.
    

    
      What Is the Build Agent?
      The Build Agent is a component of ServiceNow's App Engine ecosystem that enables automated CI/CD pipelines for applications built inside your instance. Instead of manually exporting and importing Update Sets, you define a pipeline — a series of environments and gates — and let ServiceNow handle the promotion automatically.

      It sits alongside a few other pieces you might already know:

      
        App Engine Studio (AES) — the low-code builder where your apps live
        Pipeline & Deployment spoke — the automation that moves your app between instances
        Build Agent — the worker process that actually executes the pipeline steps on a MID Server
      

      The "agent" part is important — it's not just a configuration inside ServiceNow. It's a process that runs on a MID Server and does the actual work of versioning, building, and deploying your app packages between instances. Think of it like a Jenkins agent, but baked into your ServiceNow architecture.

      
        Worth knowing: The Build Agent requires a MID Server with network access to all of your target environments. If your instances are siloed or behind strict firewalls, that's your first conversation to have before anything else.
      
    

    
      How It Actually Works
      Here's the flow from commit to production:

      
        A developer finishes work on an app in App Engine Studio on dev and clicks "Submit for deployment"
        That triggers a pipeline run — a defined sequence of stages (dev → test → prod)
        The Build Agent picks up the job via the MID Server and packages the app into a versioned artifact
        It installs that artifact into the next environment (test)
        Automated tests run — ATF test suites you've mapped to the pipeline
        If tests pass, it waits for approval (if you've configured a gate) or proceeds automatically
        Same process repeats into production
      

      The key concept here is the pipeline definition. You set this up once — define your environments, the order they get promoted through, what tests run at each stage, and whether human approval is required before moving forward. After that, every deployment follows the same path automatically.

      You can also define environment-specific properties in the pipeline so that things like connection aliases and integration credentials get swapped out correctly when the app lands in a new instance. This is huge — it means you're not manually hunting down references that point to the wrong place after a promotion.
    

    
      Getting It Set Up
      Before you can run any pipelines, you need a few things in place:

      1. A MID Server with the right plugins
      Your MID Server needs the App Engine Pipeline extensions. Install the CI/CD spoke (com.snc.cicd) on your source instance and make sure the MID Server is running a version compatible with your instance release. This is the step most people skip over in the docs and then wonder why nothing works.

      2. Credentialed connections between instances
      The pipeline needs OAuth credentials to authenticate into each target environment. You'll set up a Connection & Credential alias for each instance — dev, test, prod — and the MID Server uses those to install artifacts remotely. Use a dedicated service account for this, not your personal admin account.

      3. App Engine Studio on every instance
      AES needs to be installed on every environment in your pipeline, not just dev. This catches people off guard — the receiving instance needs to be able to accept and run the deployed package, which requires AES be present.

      4. ATF tests (optional but highly recommended)
      You don't have to have Automated Test Framework tests to use the Build Agent, but you're leaving a lot of value on the table if you don't. Set up at least basic smoke tests for your apps and map them to your pipeline stages. That's what turns this from a fancy export tool into an actual quality gate.
    

    
      Gotchas I've Hit
      Nothing in ServiceNow ever goes entirely smoothly the first time, and the Build Agent is no exception. Here's what's bitten me:

      
        MID Server connectivity — If the MID Server can't reach your target instance on port 443, the pipeline just silently fails or times out. Check your network paths first, before you touch any configuration.
        App versioning conflicts — If someone manually installed a version of the app in the target environment outside of the pipeline, you'll get version conflicts. The pipeline expects to be the single source of truth. Don't mix manual and automated deployments.
        Stale credentials — OAuth tokens expire. If a pipeline randomly fails weeks after it was working fine, check the credential alias first. Rotating the token and updating the alias usually fixes it immediately.
        Scope collisions — If your app shares a scope name with something already on the target instance (even something unrelated), the install can fail in confusing ways. Scope naming conventions matter more than you think.
        ATF environment mismatch — Tests written in dev that reference dev-specific data or users will fail in test. Build your ATF tests to be environment-agnostic or use test data that exists everywhere.
      
    

    
      My Takeaway
      The Build Agent isn't perfect, and the setup investment is real. You're configuring MID Servers, OAuth credentials, pipeline definitions, and ATF test mappings before you push a single line of code through it. That's a few days of work minimum.

      But once it's running? Deploying an app goes from a 45-minute multi-tab, multi-login manual process to clicking one button and watching it happen. The approval gates mean your change management process is built into the pipeline rather than bolted on after the fact. And the fact that every deployment follows the exact same path means you stop having those "wait, did you promote the test fix or the old version?" conversations.

      If you're running App Engine Studio and still manually promoting Update Sets, this is worth the setup time. Start with one non-critical app, get the pipeline working end to end, then expand from there.
    

    
      Get in Touch
    

    
      Helpful Resources
      
        
          ServiceNow Docs: Build Agent
          Official documentation covering installation, configuration, and pipeline setup for the Build Agent.
          Read Docs →
        

        
          App Engine Studio Overview
          Everything you need to know about App Engine Studio, the platform the Build Agent integrates with.
          Read Docs →
        

        
          CI/CD Spoke on the Store
          The ServiceNow Store listing for the CI/CD spoke — the plugin that powers the pipeline automation.
          Visit Store →
excerpt: ServiceNow's Build Agent brings real CI/CD to your instance — automated pipelines that move apps from dev to prod without the manual export and pray method.

title: Microsoft Says Password Expiry Makes You Less Secure
content:
Microsoft Says Password Expiry Makes You Less Secure
      Why forcing password changes every 90 days actually hurts your security
      
      
        December 20, 2024
        3 min read
        Beginner
        
          Security
          Best Practices
        
      
    
    
    
      
        
          📋
          What's This About?
        
        
          
            01
            
              The Mind-Blowing Revelation
              How my manager changed everything
            
          
          
            02
            
              Why Password Expiry Backfires
              The predictable human behavior
            
          
          
            03
            
              What Actually Works
              Better security approaches
            
          
          
            04
            
              My Takeaway
              Four years later, still secure
            
          
        
      
    
  

  
    
      I remember about 3 years ago my manager tapped me on the shoulder and said "You know expiring your password is actually less secure than just having a really strong password" and I noticed that this was the first place I ever worked at that didn't have a password expiration period. I was blown away and for the next 4 years working here I have never changed my password.
    

    
      The Mind-Blowing Revelation
      That conversation completely changed how I thought about password security. For years, I'd been conditioned to believe that forcing password changes every 90 days was a security best practice. Every company I'd worked for had some version of this policy - "Passwords expire every 90 days" or "You must change your password quarterly."

      But here I was, at a company that didn't force password changes at all. And according to my manager, this was actually more secure. My mind was blown.
    

    
      Why Password Expiry Backfires
      It turns out that Microsoft's research team has been saying this for years. When you force people to change passwords frequently, they do predictable things:

      
        Use patterns like Password1, Password2, Password3
        Make minimal changes like MyPassword → MyPassw0rd
        Write passwords down because they're hard to remember
        Use the same password across multiple accounts
      

      Microsoft found that users forced to change passwords every 90 days are 2.5 times more likely to use weaker passwords than users who aren't forced to change them regularly.

      The logic is simple: if an attacker gets your password, they use it immediately. They don't wait around for 90 days. And if they can't get in right away, they move on to other targets.
    

    
      What Actually Works
      Instead of forcing password changes, focus on:

      
        Strong passwords from the start - 12+ characters, unique for each account
        Multi-factor authentication (MFA) - Microsoft found this blocks 99.9% of attacks
        Monitoring for compromised passwords - change only when there's evidence of breach
        User education - teach people about password security best practices
      
    

    
      My Takeaway
      Four years later, I still haven't changed my password at that company. And you know what? I feel more secure than I ever did at places that forced quarterly changes.

      Sometimes the best security practices are the ones that work with human nature, not against it. A strong password that never changes is better than weak passwords that change every 90 days.

      What do you think? Have you worked somewhere that doesn't force password changes?
    

    
      Get in Touch
    

    
      Helpful Resources
      
        
          Microsoft Security Blog
          Official Microsoft research on password policies and security best practices.
          Read Research →
        
        
        
          NIST Password Guidelines
          National Institute of Standards and Technology guidelines on password security.
          Read Guidelines →
        
        
        
          Have I Been Pwned
          Check if your email or password has been compromised in a data breach.
          Check Your Data →
excerpt: Microsoft's research reveals that forcing password changes actually makes systems less secure. Here's why the traditional wisdom about password expiry is wrong.

title: Do We Really Need REQs in ServiceNow?
content:
Do We Really Need REQs in ServiceNow?
      Exploring when skipping Requests might work and when it definitely doesn't
      
      
        December 19, 2024
        10 min read
        Beginner
        
          ServiceNow
          Best Practices
        
      
    
    
    
      
        
          📋
          What's This About?
        
        
          
            01
            
              Quick Refresher
              REQ vs RITM vs Task hierarchy
            
          
          
            02
            
              The Client Setup
              When REQs were completely skipped
            
          
          
            03
            
              Pros & Cons Analysis
              When it works vs when it doesn't
            
          
          
            04
            
              Edge Cases & Considerations
              When skipping REQs might work
            
          
          
            05
            
              My Takeaway
              Lessons learned and recommendations
            
          
        
      
    
  

  
    
      This one's a bit of a debate, and I'm just letting the words flow. I once worked with a client who decided to skip REQs altogether. They went straight from catalog items into RITMs and Tasks, with no REQs in sight. They built their entire instance this way. At the time, I didn't fully question it, but looking back I'm still baffled. So let's play devil's advocate: is there ever a case where a REQ just isn't necessary?
    

    
      Quick Refresher: REQ vs RITM vs Task
      For the newcomers:
      
      Let's talk about what makes up a Request in ServiceNow.
      
      
        
          
            Request (REQ)
            #REQ0012345
            The best way I was taught to visualize this is to think about it as your Amazon receipt. The REQ is the order number.
          
        

        ↓

        
          
            
              Requested Item (RITM)
              #RITM0001
              Laptop, the specific item ordered
            
          

          
            
              Requested Item (RITM)
              #RITM0002
              Monitor, the specific item ordered
            
          
        

        ↓

        
          
            
              Task
              #TASK0001
              Procurement, order the hardware
            
          

          
            
              Task
              #TASK0002
              Setup, configure the equipment
            
          

          
            
              Task
              #TASK0003
              Delivery, deliver to user
            
          
        
      

      Normally, it's a clean hierarchy: one REQ, multiple RITMs under it, and then Tasks under each RITM.

      
    

    
      The Client's Setup
      Here's what my client did: instead of having separate catalog items for different office equipment, they created one giant "office equipment" catalog item. Need a monitor? Check the box. Need a chair? Check the box. Need a docking station? Same form.

      When submitted, instead of spinning up a REQ that contained those RITMs, they went directly into RITMs with all the selections stored as variables. Essentially, they built an order guide without actually using the order guide functionality that ServiceNow gives you.

      On the surface, this worked. Orders flowed, tasks were created, and fulfillment teams still got the work. So what's the harm, right?
    

    
      Pros and Cons: Skipping REQs in ServiceNow
      
      
        
          Pros of Skipping REQs
          
            
              ✓
              
                Simplicity for End Users
                End users don't care about record hierarchy. They just want their request completed. Cutting REQs out of the equation might look like it's simplifying things.
              
            
            
            
              ✓
              
                Less "Clicking Around"
                With fewer records in play, some admins think it'll be easier to manage or faster to submit. One record instead of three? Sounds efficient.
              
            
            
            
              ✓
              
                Custom Catalog Design
                If a client already has a "do-it-all" catalog item, putting everything into variables seems like a shortcut to avoid managing dozens of smaller items or maintaining order guides.
              
            
          
        
        
        
          Cons of Skipping REQs
          
            
              ✗
              
                Loss of Hierarchy & Traceability
                REQs are the umbrella. Without them, you lose the ability to group multiple RITMs under one logical "order." Imagine someone ordering a laptop, monitor, and phone. There's no single "parent" record to tie those items together.
              
            
            
            
              ✗
              
                Reporting Becomes Painful
                Without REQs, reporting has to be done at the variable level. That's not intuitive, and it requires advanced reporting skills. Want to know how many office chairs were ordered last quarter? Good luck, it's buried in variables instead of clear RITMs under a REQ.
              
            
            
            
              ✗
              
                Approvals Get Messy
                REQs provide a clean approval structure. Without them, you're forcing approvals to happen at the RITM level or building custom logic. Approving a "bundle" of items becomes awkward when there's no parent record.
              
            
            
            
              ✗
              
                Auditing and Compliance
                Many organizations need a clear audit trail. The REQ record shows who requested what, when, and how it was fulfilled. Without that, you risk compliance gaps.
              
            
            
            
              ✗
              
                Scalability Issues
                What works for a small catalog breaks at scale. As the number of requests grows, managing fulfillment, SLAs, and reporting all without REQs becomes unmanageable.
              
            
            
            
              ✗
              
                Integration Challenges
                If you ever integrate ServiceNow with procurement systems, ERP, or asset management, those systems expect a clear request structure. Skipping REQs makes integration way harder.
              
            
          
        
      
    

    
      Devil's Advocate: Could Skipping REQs Ever Work?
      Okay, let's be fair here. While I've been pretty harsh on skipping REQs, there might be some edge cases where it's not completely insane. Let me walk through the scenarios where someone might actually consider it:

      Niche Use Cases
      If your catalog item is hyper-specific, always produces exactly one RITM, and doesn't need grouping or approvals, then skipping REQs might not be catastrophic.
      Example: A simple password reset request that goes straight to the help desk.

      Proof of Concept / Early Stage
      A small organization testing ServiceNow for the first time might hack around REQs just to get things moving quickly.
      Example: A 50-person company with 3 catalog items, no complex workflows.

      Custom Apps Outside ITSM
      If you're using ServiceNow for something unconventional (say, a lightweight intake form), you could argue that REQs add unnecessary overhead.
      Example: A feedback form that creates records but doesn't need the full request lifecycle.

      But Here's the Reality Check
      Even in these "edge cases," you're usually better off keeping REQs. Here's why:
      
        Future-proofing: The moment you need real reporting, scaling, or compliance, you'll regret skipping them
        Platform consistency: ServiceNow is designed around this hierarchy - fighting it just creates technical debt
        Maintenance overhead: Custom workarounds require ongoing maintenance and documentation
        Team knowledge: New team members expect standard ServiceNow patterns
      
      Bottom line: While these scenarios might seem like exceptions, they're really just different flavors of technical debt waiting to happen.
    

    
      My Takeaway
      At the time I worked with that client, I didn't think their setup was all that bad. It worked, requests got fulfilled, and the system didn't break.

      But now, with more experience, I see how much harder they made things for themselves: reporting was clunky, approvals were inconsistent, and scaling the catalog meant bending ServiceNow in unnatural ways. Looking back, I'm still baffled that their implementation partners didn't fight harder to stop them from going down this route.

      Here's the thing about ServiceNow: its beauty lies in its incredible flexibility. You can do almost anything in so many different ways. The platform is so customizable that you can bend it to fit almost any workflow or process you can imagine. But that's also its biggest challenge.

      The hardest part isn't making ServiceNow do what you want - it's choosing a path that works for you today but won't cause you headaches in the future. Every customization, every workaround, every "creative solution" comes with a cost. Sometimes that cost is immediate, sometimes it's hidden until you try to scale or maintain the system.

      So, is there ever a point where a REQ just isn't necessary? Maybe in theory. But in practice, REQs are part of the backbone of ServiceNow for a reason. They bring order, reporting clarity, and compliance in a way that skipping them just can't. The question isn't whether you can skip them - it's whether you should.

      What do you think? Are REQs always worth it, or have you seen a scenario where skipping them actually worked?
    

    
      Get in Touch
    

    
      Helpful Resources
      
        
          Order Guides
          Official ServiceNow documentation on Order Guides and how they create the proper REQ/RITM structure.
          Read Documentation →
        
        
        
          Catalog Builder Overview
          Learn about ServiceNow's Catalog Builder and how to create effective catalog items.
          Read Documentation →
        
        
        
          Record Producer Knowledge Base
          Detailed information about Record Producers and extended catalog item functions in ServiceNow.
          Read Documentation →
excerpt: A deep dive into whether ServiceNow Requests (REQs) are always necessary, exploring the pros and cons of skipping them and when it might actually work.
